questions.q: What was Maxwell's great theoretical achievement that Hertz set out to test?
options: Unifying electricity and magnetism into one theory and predicting that light is a self-propagating electromagnetic wave
Measuring the charge on a single electron
questions.explanation: Maxwell compressed all known electricity and magnetism into a unified set of equations and predicted self-sustaining electromagnetic waves — concluding that light itself is such a wave. He died before any were generated or detected.

questions.q: Which term that Maxwell added to the equations made self-propagating electromagnetic waves possible?
options: The displacement current — a changing electric field, even with no current, creates a magnetic field
questions.explanation: Maxwell's new displacement current term said a changing electric field alone creates a magnetic field. Combined with Faraday's law (a changing magnetic field creates an electric field), each field continually regenerates the other, allowing a wave to propagate through vacuum.

questions.q: Maxwell calculated the speed of an electromagnetic wave from two constants. Which expression did he use?
questions.explanation: Maxwell found \(v = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}\), where \(\mu_0\) is the permeability and \(\epsilon_0\) the permittivity of free space. Substituting their measured values gave ~\(3\times10^8\) m/s — the speed of light, from constants measured in jars and coils.

questions.q: Why was the match between Maxwell's calculated speed and the measured speed of light so significant?
options: Because the two values were completely different
Because the speed came from electric and magnetic constants having nothing to do with optics, yet equalled the speed of light, implying light is an electromagnetic wave
Because it proved light was made of particles
Because it showed light needed a medium to travel
questions.explanation: The speed emerged from constants measured in electrical experiments unrelated to light, yet matched the measured speed of light to within a percent or two. This could not be coincidence: light must be an electromagnetic wave, and an invisible spectrum of other frequencies must exist.

questions.q: How did Hertz's spark-gap oscillator (the transmitter) produce electromagnetic waves?
options: By heating a wire until it glowed
By making charge oscillate rapidly back and forth across the rods — accelerating charge radiates electromagnetic waves
By rotating a magnet near a coil
By passing a steady direct current through a long wire
questions.explanation: When the spark jumped, the stored charge surged back and forth along the rod millions of times per second. Each oscillation is an accelerating charge, and Maxwell's theory says accelerating charges radiate electromagnetic waves.

questions.q: What physical principle explains how Hertz's loop-antenna receiver detected the waves?
options: Faraday's law — the wave's changing magnetic field through the loop induces a voltage that drives a tiny spark across the gap
questions.explanation: The wave's oscillating magnetic field passes through the loop's area; a changing magnetic flux through a loop induces a voltage (Faraday's law). Tuned to resonance, this induced voltage builds up enough to spark across the micrometer gap.

questions.q: Hertz measured the wavelength of his waves using a standing wave between his transmitter and a zinc reflector. What is the spacing between adjacent nodes?
questions.explanation: The outgoing and reflected waves superimpose into a standing wave. Adjacent nodes (where the field is always zero and the receiver spark vanishes) are spaced half a wavelength apart, so measuring node-to-node spacing gives \(\lambda/2\).

questions.q: Once Hertz had the wavelength (~4 m) and the frequency of his oscillator, how did he obtain the wave speed?
options: By timing the wave across the room with a stopwatch
Using \(v = f\lambda\), multiplying the frequency by the measured wavelength
questions.explanation: The universal wave equation \(v = f\lambda\) gives the speed. Hertz measured \(\lambda\) from the node spacing and calculated \(f\) from the oscillator's L-C resonance; multiplying them gave roughly \(3\times10^8\) m/s, the speed of light.

questions.q: Hertz showed his rotating receiver loop killed the spark at one orientation and restored it at another. Which property of the waves did this demonstrate?
options: Polarisation, confirming the waves are transverse
questions.explanation: Only a transverse wave can be polarised. Rotating the loop changed how the wave's transverse fields couple to it, killing or restoring the spark — confirming the waves are transverse, exactly as Maxwell predicted for light.

questions.q: All electromagnetic waves — from gamma rays to radio waves — share which property in a vacuum?
options: They all have the same wavelength
They all have the same frequency
They all travel at the same speed, \(c \approx 3\times10^8\) m/s, differing only in frequency and wavelength
They all require a medium to propagate
questions.explanation: Maxwell's theory allows waves of any frequency, but all travel at the same speed \(c\) in a vacuum, related by \(c = f\lambda\). They differ only in frequency and wavelength; in Maxwell's day only visible light and infrared had been observed.
